request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"rethinkdb::devtools","name":"rethinkdb","one_liner":"Free, open-source distributed document database with real-time change feeds, unable to convert developer adoption into a sustainable commercial business.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":1,"rationale":"The new pitch is a per-driver B2B SaaS subscription with usage overages — a clear recurring-revenue model targeting paying SMB/mid-market customers. RethinkDB was free, open-source software with no stated monetization model; Trusted facts list its price point as 'free' and monetization as 'other'. These are fundamentally incompatible business models."},"market":{"score":1,"rationale":"The new pitch targets US regional 3PLs (logistics/supply chain). RethinkDB targeted global developers needing a distributed database (devtools/infra). Completely different verticals, buyer personas, and competitive landscapes."},"gtm":{"score":1,"rationale":"The new pitch implies a direct sales or inside-sales motion to logistics operators. RethinkDB relied on open-source community adoption and developer grassroots growth — a PLG model with no direct-sales component described. No meaningful GTM overlap."},"stage_scale":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both are early-stage ventures seeking product-market fit before achieving scale. RethinkDB was seed-funded (YC 2009) and operated ~7 years without cracking commercial scale. This is the only loose parallel — both are pre-scale companies — but stage similarity alone is minimal."}},"why_similar":"The only meaningful surface-level overlap is that both are software products solving a workflow/data problem for a technical-ish user base and both seek recurring value delivery. Both also struggled or would struggle with the challenge of converting a narrow, specialized product into sustainable revenue before runway runs out.","where_diverged":"Virtually every strategic dimension diverges. (1) Monetization: the new pitch charges per driver plus usage overages from day one; RethinkDB was perpetually free/open-source and never established a commercial price. (2) Customer type: the pitch targets logistics operators (3PL businesses), not software developers. (3) Geography: the pitch is US-focused; RethinkDB was global. (4) Sector: logistics SaaS vs. distributed database devtools. (5) Business model: direct B2B SaaS subscription vs. open-source with no clear monetization path. These are not minor differences — the two companies share almost no strategic DNA.","failure_causes":["inability to monetize open-source adoption","no sustainable commercial revenue model","free price point with no paid tier or enterprise upsell described","developer/community adoption did not convert to paying customers","competition from better-funded or more mature database alternatives","failure to differentiate sufficiently to command pricing power","runway exhaustion before product-market fit in commercial segment"],"lessons_for_input":["Lock in per-driver pricing and usage-overage terms before launch — never let your core product become implicitly free in customers' minds.","Validate willingness-to-pay with at least 3 paying 3PL pilots before scaling headcount or infrastructure.","Build commercial contracts (not just pilots) early; open adoption without revenue is a ticking clock on runway.","Ensure your route-optimizer and dispatcher cockpit deliver measurable, dollar-quantifiable ROI so renewal conversations are data-driven, not relationship-driven.","Avoid over-engineering the product for edge cases before you have paying customers — RethinkDB shipped a production-ready v2.0 in 2015 but still could not build a business around it."]}
